coredata.dc:title: Integrating N doped KNbO3 tailored with Sr2V2O7 for escalated performance towards photocatalytic degradation of metronidazole: Insights into mechanism, degradation pathway and its toxicity evaluation
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Khan, S. Sudheer
Subhiksha, V.
Harini, G.
Syed, Asad
Janani, B.
Steffy, J.P.
Elgorban, Abdallah M.
Abid, Islem
Wong, Ling Shing
coredata.dc:description: The presence of antibiotics poses a significant environmental challenge due to their resistance to natural degradation. In this study, we employ state-of-the-art pristine nanomaterials including nitrogen (N) doped potassium niobate (KNbO3), and strontium pyrovanadate (Sr2V2O7) to intricately engineer N-KNbO3/Sr2V2O7 nanocomposites (KS NCs) for boosted photocatalytic degradation of metronidazole (MTZ). The microscopic techniques were used to reveal the nanocluster morphology, amorphous nature, proper dopant distribution and heterojunction formation. While X-Ray diffraction studies confirmed the purity of the synthesized materials. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy validated their chemical states and bonding nature. Brunauer–Emmett–Teller revealed the enhanced surface area and mesoporous nature of the NCs. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy was used to demonstrate in the successful visible-light sensitization of the KS NCs with a bandgap of 2.80 eV. Further, PL studies indicated the reduced charge carrier recombination in the NCs and ESR proved the production of O2 •− and •OH during photocatalysis. The photocatalytic degradation of MTZ by KS NCs attained 96.2 % in 140 min, and the effective results noted at pH 6. Moreover, these NCs efficacy was not reduced by the presence of ions and it demonstrated exceptional stability over multiple operational cycles. The application of gas chromatography-mass spectroscopy analysis allowed for the identification of potential degradation pathways of MTZ, denotes the formation of less harmful end products. Overall, this investigation showcases the significant potential of KS NCs for elimination of organic pollutants in water and paves a strategic way for manufacturing innovation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photodegradation
N–KNbO3/Sr2V2O7 nanocomposites
Metronidazole
GC-MS/MS analysis
Wastewater treatment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Band gap and band edge potentials
Influence of photocatalyst and MTZ dosage
Effect of pH and ionic environment
Possible photocatalytic degradation pathway of MTZ

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the synthesis of nanomaterials.
SEM image of (a)KNbO3, (b) Sr2V2O7 NPs and (c) KS-1 NCs. (d) Elemental mapping of KS-1 NCs.
(a) XPS survey of KS-1 NCs and high-resolution binding energy spectra of (b) N 1s, (c) K 2p, (d) Nb3d, (e) V 2p and (f) O 1s.
(a)BET and BJH (inset) plots, (b) PL spectra, (c) EIS spectra and (d) Kubelka–Munk plot of KNbO3, Sr2V2O7 NPs, and KS-1 NCs.
(a) UV-Visible spectra demonstrating photodegradation of MTZ, (b) plot of C/C0 vs time and (c) ln(C/C0) vs time.
Proposed photocatalytic mechanism for MTZ degradation using KS-1NCs.
Photodegradation pathway and intermediate compounds predicted using GC-MS/MS analysis.

head.ce:title: Integrating N doped KNbO3 tailored with Sr2V2O7 for escalated performance towards photocatalytic degradation of metronidazole: Insights into mechanism, degradation pathway and its toxicity evaluation

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: fFaculty of Health and Life Sciences, INTI International University, Putra Nilai, 71800 Nilai, Negeri Sembilan, Malaysia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The presence of antibiotics poses a significant environmental challenge due to their resistance to natural degradation. In this study, we employ state-of-the-art pristine nanomaterials including nitrogen (N) doped potassium niobate (KNbO3), and strontium pyrovanadate (Sr2V2O7) to intricately engineer N-KNbO3/Sr2V2O7 nanocomposites (KS NCs) for boosted photocatalytic degradation of metronidazole (MTZ). The microscopic techniques were used to reveal the nanocluster morphology, amorphous nature, proper dopant distribution and heterojunction formation. While X-Ray diffraction studies confirmed the purity of the synthesized materials. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy and Raman spectroscopy validated their chemical states and bonding nature. Brunauer–Emmett–Teller revealed the enhanced surface area and mesoporous nature of the NCs. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy was used to demonstrate in the successful visible-light sensitization of the KS NCs with a bandgap of 2.80 eV. Further, PL studies indicated the reduced charge carrier recombination in the NCs and ESR proved the production of O2 •− and •OH during photocatalysis. The photocatalytic degradation of MTZ by KS NCs attained 96.2 % in 140 min, and the effective results noted at pH 6. Moreover, these NCs efficacy was not reduced by the presence of ions and it demonstrated exceptional stability over multiple operational cycles. The application of gas chromatography-mass spectroscopy analysis allowed for the identification of potential degradation pathways of MTZ, denotes the formation of less harmful end products. Overall, this investigation showcases the significant potential of KS NCs for elimination of organic pollutants in water and paves a strategic way for manufacturing innovation.

ce:section.ce:para: The presence of antibiotics in aquatic system is a great concern for both public wellness and environmental sustainability. The issue arises from the widespread use of antibiotics in various sectors, including healthcare, agriculture, and veterinary practices [1]. The introduction of antibiotics into aquatic ecosystems has the capacity to disturb the equilibrium of microbial communities, potentially impacting the balance of microorganisms [2]. Metronidazole (MTZ) is a widely utilized antibiotic with both antibacterial and anti-inflammatory properties and primarily employed as a remedy for anaerobic bacteria and protozoa infection and MTZ is relatively stable in the environment so it can persist for extended periods. This stability increases the likelihood of accumulation in soil and water bodies [3]. Concerns have been raised about the potential carcinogenic and mutagenic properties of MTZ owing to its nitro group in the nitroimidazole ring within its chemical structure [4]. The nitro group can undergo metabolic activation, forming reactive intermediates that may interact with cellular components and potentially lead to genotoxic effects and also it can disrupt the balance of microbial communities, affecting water quality and the health of aquatic ecosystems [5].

Various conventional physical and chemical methods have been utilized to eliminate antibiotics from aquatic environments, which include microbial degradation [6], electrochemical techniques, physical adsorption, and ozonation [7]. Therefore, there is a strong need for an efficient technique to eliminate these antibiotics from water sources. Photocatalysis presents a versatile approach applicable to various pollutants, offering broad applicability and it can break down antibiotics into simpler and less harmful compounds [8]. Photocatalysts are stable and reusable, reducing the need for chemical additives and minimizing secondary pollution. The technique is energy-efficient, scalable, and capable of rapid degradation of pollutants [9].

Potassium niobate (KNbO3) emerges as a promising candidate for the photocatalytic oxidation due to its advantageous blend of non-toxicity, robust photocatalytic stability and a distinctive energy band structure [10]. KNbO3 have shown efficacy in a range of applications including the hydrogen production [11], nitrogen fixation [12], reduction of CO2 [13], and organic waste degradation [14]. However, the challenge is restricted absorption of white light due to the wide band gap (approximately 3.20 eV) of KNbO3, impeding the efficacy as a photocatalyst. de Moraes et al. [15] reported the use of a TiO2/KNbO3/g–C3N4 composite as a photocatalyst for the degradation of sulfamerazine, achieving a degradation rate of 90 % within 5 h. Iwase et al. [16] detailed the development of an Ir and Sr-co-doped KNbO3 photocatalyst, which was employed as an O2-evolving photocatalyst for Z-scheme water splitting under visible light irradiation. Additionally, Yu et al. [17] demonstrated a significant enhancement in photocatalytic H2 production using a KNbO3 photocatalyst that was modified through carbon doping and MoS2 decoration. These diverse applications underscore the versatile capabilities of KNbO3 as a photocatalyst, highlighting its potential for use in various photocatalytic processes ranging from pollutant degradation to hydrogen production and water splitting.

Strontium pyro-vanadate (Sr2V2O7) is another complex metal oxide whose photocatalytic efficiency has not been extensively studied. At elevated temperatures, it exhibits a triclinic crystal structure, whereas at lower temperatures, it adopts a tetragonal β-phase. The presence of VO4 dimers can influence the electronic structure of the material, affecting its photoluminescent properties [18,19]. Recent electronic structure calculations have provided intriguing insights into the photocatalytic potential of Sr2V2O7. These calculations reveal that the valence band of Sr2V2O7 is primarily composed of oxygen atoms, while the conduction band is mainly formed by the d orbitals of vanadium atoms [20]. Notably, the 3d orbital of vanadium lies below the d orbitals of some other transition metals, resulting in a lower energy level for the conduction band. This unique electronic configuration suggests that Sr2V2O7 could be an excellent candidate for efficient electron–hole (e⁻/h⁺) separation [21].

Doping of non-metals or metals on nanoparticles can be an effective strategy to mitigate the recombination rate e−/h+ pairs consequently improving the separation efficiency [22]. Nitrogen doping is known to modify the material's electronic structure by generating new energy levels which can result in improved optical and electronic properties, especially in the visible light range [23,24]. The novelty of this study lies in the doping of nitrogen on KNbO3 with Sr2V2O7 as a photocatalyst to degrade MTZ under visible light irradiation. We focus on employing photocatalytic degradation methods utilizing versatile materials such as KNbO3 and Sr2V2O7 to tackle the intricate issue of antibiotic pollution in aquatic environments.

Niobium pentoxide (Nb2O5, 99.9 %) was purchased in Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd, India. Sulphuric acid (H2SO4, 98 %), potassium hydroxide (KOH, 85 %) and strontium nitrate (Sr(NO3)2, 99 %) were purchased from Merk, India. Ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3, 99 %) and urea (NH2CO NH2, 99 %) were purchased from Sisco research laboratory Pvt. Ltd, India All the compounds obtained were of analytical grade and used without any further modification.

The KNbO3 nanoparticles (NPs) were synthesized as per the previous literature with a slight modification [25]. Initially, 1 g of Nb2O5 was dissolved in 2 mL of concentrated H2SO4 and made up to 20 mL. Following, 36 g of KOH was added in 40 mL of d.H2O and was introduced to the Nb2O5–H2SO4 drop wise under stirring until the pH reach 10. The resulting mixture underwent stirring for a duration of 30 min. Then, the prepared solution was poured to a Teflon lined stainless-steel autoclave and kept at constant temperature of 180 °C for a period of 6 h. This controlled hydrothermal process facilitated the formation of KNbO3 nanoparticles with desired properties. The KNbO3 NPs were centrifuged and washed several times with H2O and C2H5OH and dried at 80 °C for 12 h. N–KNbO3 was prepared by the addition of 0.6 g of urea to 1 g of KNbO3 and calcined at 400 °C for 2 h under inert atmosphere.

Sr2V2O7 decorated N–KNbO3 nanocomposites (NCs) were prepared through an in-situ ultrasonication method. Initially, 0.5 mM Sr(NO3)2 was added to 1 mM NH4VO3 in 40 mL distilled water for the preparation of Sr2V2O7. Followed by adding 0.1 g N–KNbO3 and ultrasonicated for another 30 min. The dispersed solution underwent magnetic stirring for a duration of 1 h to facilitate the formation of the desired NCs and the resulting NCs were then poured to Teflon lined stainless-steel autoclave, and kept at 160 °C for a period of 14 h. This controlled thermal environment and duration were chosen to optimize the synthesis process and enhance the characteristics of NCs. Thereafter, the solution was centrifuged and the pellet was treated at 60 °C for 8 h. Here, three different molar percentage of composite was prepared by adding required amount of Sr(NO3)2 and NH4VO3 in order to achieve 10, 20 and 30 % and were named as N–KNbO3/Sr2V2O7-10 % (KS-1), N–KNbO3/Sr2V2O7-20 % (KS-2), and N–KNbO3/Sr2V2O7-30 % (KS-3) respectively. The schematic representation for the synthesis of N–KNbO3/ Sr2V2O7 NCs is given in Fig. 1 .

The particles were subjected to field-emission scanning electron microscopic (FE-SEM) analysis by utilizing a JEOL, Japan. Energy dispersive X-ray (EDAX) analysis by using performed to understand the elements present in the material. High-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) analysis was conducted with a Carl–Zeiss instrument, Germany. These sophisticated analytical techniques allowed for detailed characterization and imaging of the synthesized materials, providing valuable insights into their structural and elemental properties. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra were obtained using a Bruker D8 Advance instrument operating at 40 kV and 40 mA with Cu Kα radiation. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) analysis was conducted using an PerkinElmer USA. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained by using the Thermo Scientific™ ESCALAB™ Xi+ instrument, USA. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded by utilizing FLS 1000 instrument, Edinburgh. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopic (DRS) was conducted using a Perkin–Elmer Lambda 750 instrument. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) analysis was performed by using Thermo Fisher Scientific Nicolet IS50, USA. A Shimadzu UV-visible spectrophotometer was employed to measure absorbance spectra in the 200–900 nm range.

The appropriate concentrations of photocatalyst and MTZ were dispersed in water such that the final volume of the reaction mixture remained 20 mL. The reaction mixture underwent a 30 min incubation period in a shaker under lightless conditions to establish the adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Subsequently, the containers containing the reaction mixtures were exposed to a 1000 W halogen lamp. At regular intervals of 20 min, 2 mL samples were collected, and their absorbance at λmax = 324 nm was recorded after centrifugation. Thereafter, the supernatant was mixed with the pellet, and the combined solution was returned to the original beaker. The percentage of degradation was determined using formula (1), calculated as follows:
(1)
Degradation
percentage
( % )
=
(
A 0
− A
A 0
)
× 100
where A0 implies initial absorbance (at t = 0) and A implies absorbance value recorded at time t. The study aimed to explore the influence of various NCs doses (ranging from 20 to 60 mg/L) and varied dosages of the drug MTZ (ranging from 15 to 35 mg/L). Additionally, the impact of diverse pH levels (ranging from 3 to 10) and the presence of various anions (NO3 −, SO4 2−, Cl−, PO4 − and CO3 2−) was also examined using the same experimental procedure. This comprehensive investigation allowed for a thorough understanding of how these factors affect the overall efficiency of the process. Here, the pH in the reaction mixture was accomplished using 1 N HCl and 1 N NaOH, monitored with a pH meter. Anions were incorporated to achieve a final concentration of 1 mM in the reaction mixture. To assess the recyclability of the photocatalyst, a series of six consecutive cycles were conducted. Following each cycle, the gathered NCs were dispersed into a fresh reaction mixture containing the target drug (MTZ). The stability of the NCs and the potential effects of photo-corrosion were scrutinized through X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses of the used catalyst. For radical scavenging experiments, specific compounds were introduced into the reaction mixture: 1 mM benzoquinone (BQ), 0.1mL tert-butyl alcohol (TBA), 1 mM ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA) and 1 mM silver nitrate (AgNO3). These additions, resulting in a final volume of 20 mL, were intended to capture superoxide ( . O2-) and hydroxyl (·OH) radicals, and h+ and e− respectively. The experimental setup was designed to explore the influence of these scavengers on the photocatalytic process.

In Fig. 2 a, the SEM image of KNbO3 depicts the formation of uniform, cube-shaped particles synthesized through a gradual hydrothermal process. Fig. 2b illustrates the nanorod-like Sr2V2O7 NPs, while Fig. 2c shows the presence of nanorod embedded with nano cube -like morphology. For further insight, HR-TEM image of KS-1 NCs were captured and shown in Supplementary materials Fig. S1a and b. The TEM images confirm the formation of nano-cubes with highly sharp edges and uniform nature. The lattice d-space corresponding to both counter parts reveal merging interface within the KS-1 NCs, indicative of interface between pristine counter parts. Supplementary materials Fig. S1c displays the SAED pattern of KS-1 NCs, showing dotted rings characteristic predominantly with ring structures owing to the NCs' major part as single crystalline nature along with overall high crystallinity. Additionally, elemental mapping depicted in Fig. 2d indicate the presence of potassium (K), niobium (Nb), oxygen (O), strontium (Sr), vanadium (V), and nitrogen (N) in KS-1 NCs devoid of any impurities. In conclusion, these findings confirm the uniform distribution of dopants and the formation of intact interface within the KS-1 NCs.

XRD pattern of KNbO3, Sr2V2O7, N–KNbO3/Sr2V2O7 and N–KNbO3/Sr2V2O7 (reused) is depicted in Fig. 3 . For KNbO3 NPs, bands at 22.15º, 31.47º, 45.06º, 50.82º, 56.10º, 65.70º, 70.23º, 74.67º, 79.07º and 83.48º matched with the (100), (110), (200), (210), (211), (220), (300), (310), (311) and (222) hkl planes (JCPDS card. No. 00-008-0212). For Sr2V2O7 NPs, bands at 12.80º, 14.10º, 19.67º, 25.20º, 26.90º, 29.68º, 35.07º, 41.23º and 43.24º that correspond to the (101), (002), (201), (011), (111), (303), (205) and (403) facets of orthorhombic phase of Sr2V2O7 (JCPDS Card. No. 00-028-1268). The diffraction spectra of N–KNbO3/Sr2V2O7 and N–KNbO3/Sr2V2O7 (reused) has all corresponding peaks of pristine KNbO3 and Sr2V2O7 NPs with no extra peaks of impurities.

XPS survey spectrum of N–KNbO3/Sr2V2O7 NCs analysis is shown in Fig. 4 a. The N 1s spectra were deconvoluted to 400.6, 401.5, 403.0, 403.9 and 405.1 eV corresponded to the charged nitrogen of bipolaron and polaron (N–H+ and –N–H+), neutral nitrogen (–N–H– structures), imine (N– and –N–) structures [26]. In V 2p, the peaks at 521.3 and 522.8 eV ascribed to V4+ and V5+ valence states of V 2p1/2 respectively while peaks at 527.9 and 528.9 eV ascribed to the V4+ and V5+ valence states of V 2p3/2 respectively (Fig. 4c) [27]. In wide spectrum of K 2p (Fig. 4d), the peaks at 292.2 and 289.4 eV attributed to K 2p1/2 and K 2p3/2 along with their satellite peaks at 293.3 and 290.3 eV respectively [28]. The Nb 3d wide spectrum as shown in Fig. 4e were deconvoluted into Nb 3d3/2 and Nb 3d5/2 located at 204.7 and 207.4 eV respectively [29]. The Sr 3d wide spectra (Fig. 4f) depicts the Sr 3d3/2 and Sr 3d5/2 at 132.7 and 131 eV respectively. The high-resolution O 1s spectra shown in Fig. 4g having two peaks at 529.5and 527.9 eV ascribed to the V–O bond and chemisorbed surface oxygen respectively.

Surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) analysis result is shown in Supplementary materials Fig. S2, offering significant information regarding the bonding characteristics and chemical composition of the photocatalyst. From the Raman modes of KNbO3 and KS-1 NCs, the peaks at 191, 244, 277, 280, 295, 533, 600, and 832 cm−1 affirms the presence of orthorhombic phase of KNbO3. Especially, modes at 191, 277, 280 and 295 cm−1 were attributed to the O–Nb–O symmetric stretching vibration. Similarly, the peaks at 533, and 600 cm−1 ascribed to Nb–O symmetric stretching vibration. Lastly, the peak at 832 cm−1 affirms the combination modes of O–Nb–O and Nb–O symmetric stretching vibrations [30,31]. Likewise, the Raman spectra of Sr2V2O7 and KS-1 NCs depicts all ascribed symmetric stretching vibrations belonging to Sr2V2O7 NPs. The peaks present in ranges of 950–800 cm−1, 800 to 400 cm−1 and below 400 cm−1 were corresponded to the symmetric V–O stretching vibrations, V–O–V band bending vibrations and peripheral modes of vibrations respectively [25].

Surface area and pore analysis of the photocatalyst were examined using BET and BJH techniques (Supplementary materials Table S1). The results indicated that the surface area followed the sequence of KS-1 NCs > Sr2V2O7 > KNbO3, in which KS-1 NCs exhibiting the highest surface area among their individual components. The photocatalyst followed type III isotherm. The inset in Fig. 5 a illustrates the pore size distribution, showing that KS-1 NCs have a pore radius of 1.483 nm and a pore volume of 0.129 cc/g.

For the optoelectronic properties of the NCs, various insights can be obtained from the utilization of PL spectroscopy. This technique enables a comprehensive analysis of the material's behaviour concerning the recombination rate of e−/h+ pairs subsequent to light-induced excitation. Enhanced PL intensity corresponds to accelerated charge carrier recombination. As evidenced in Fig. 5b, the emission intensities are ranked in the order of KNbO3> Sr2V2O7> KS-1 NCs. Remarkably, KS-1 NCs exhibit substantially reduced PL intensity as representation of effective visible-light sensitization and proficient suppression of charge carrier recombination.

To understand the charge transfer resistance of the fabricated photocatalsyt, electrochemical impedance spectroscopic (EIS) analysis was also employed. Fig. 5c presents the Nyquist plot comparing the counter parts with KS-1 NCs. Typically, the high-frequency semicircle indicates the resistance rate (Rct) against charge transfer. Thus, a smaller semicircle suggests reduced resistance and, consequently, enhanced separation of photogenerated charges. Notably, KS-1 NCs exhibit a significantly smaller arc radius than its pristine parts, as depicted in Fig. 5c. This suggests that KS-1 NCs effectively facilitate the separation and mobility of e−/h+, thereby demonstrating superior efficiency. These findings from EIS analysis corroborate the conclusions drawn from PL analysis, confirming the efficacy of the nano-heterojunction as a proficient photocatalyst for the MTZ degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Band gap and band edge potentials
ce:section.ce:para:
The UV–vis-DRS studies revealed that N-doped KNbO3 exhibits a transition in absorption from the UV region to visible light. Notably, the intensity of light absorption experiences a significant enhancement upon N-doping of KNbO3 in the visible region, signifying an improved optical response of the catalyst. The determination of nanomaterials' bandgap was carried out using the Kubelka-Munk (KM) function, as illustrated in Fig. 5d. The KM equation associated with this function is expressed by Eq (2):
(2) F(R) hν2 = A (hν – Eg)
where F(R) represents KM function, Eg is the band gap energy, ν is the incident photons frequency and A is a constant value. The bandgap values of N–KNbO3, Sr2V2O7 and Sr2V2O7/N–KNbO3 NCs was 2.55, 2.96 and 2.80 eV respectively, determined through UV–vis-DRS analysis. Subsequently, Mulliken's electronegativity theory was employed to determine the band edge potentials using Eqs (3) and (4):
(3) EVB = χ – Ec + 0.5 Eg
(4) ECB = EVB–Eg
where, EVB ​ and ECB denote the potentials of the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB), respectively. The standard potential of the hydrogen electrode is denoted by Ec​ (with a value of 4.5 eV), Eg​ signifies the band gap of the semiconductor, and χ represents the absolute electronegativity of the catalyst. Consequently, the calculated values of CB and VB for KNbO3 were –0.52 and 2.01 eV, and for Sr2V2O7, they were –0.43 and 2.52 eV, respectively.

An investigation on photocatalysis efficiency was conducted under white-light irradiation. MTZ was employed as a model pollutant. The absorption spectra of MTZ (20 mg/L) demonstrated a gradual decrease with increasing exposure time to light, indicating the photocatalytic activity of KS-1 NCs (30 mg/L) (Fig. 6 a). This diminishing absorption peak intensity signifies the degradation of MTZ within the reaction mixture. Comparison of KNbO3, Sr2V2O7, KS-1, KS-2, and KS-3 NCs, calculated at λmax = 324 nm, revealed distinct degradation rates (Fig. 6b). The photodegradation of MTZ by pristine KNbO3 and Sr2V2O7 reached 83.2 and 52.0 % respectively. On the construction of heterojunction by combining KNbO3 and Sr2V2O7 NPs, the photocatalytic activity of KS-2 and KS-3 NCs were 61.5 and 88.7 % respectively. Out of all, KS-1 NCs achieved a maximum of 96.2 % MTZ removal in 140 min.

The degradation rate of MTZ was analysed in this study by plotting ln C/C0 vs time (Fig. 6c). Experimental findings consistently matched a pseudo-first-order kinetics model, with correlation constants (R2) exceeding 0.9 in all cases. Initially, the rate was notably accelerated in KS-1 NCs, owing to the heterojunction formation between KNbO3 and Sr2V2O7. The rate constant for KS-1 NCs (k = 0.022 min−1) was outperformed than other percentages. This indicating an enhancement likely due to the optimal number of adsorption sites promoting rapid charge transfer and efficient molecular transformation pathways, thereby effectively reducing e-/h+ recombination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of photocatalyst and MTZ dosage
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of initial NCs dose was evaluated by varying concentrations of KS-1 NCs from 20 to 60 mg/L (supplementary material Fig. S3a and b). Results indicated the increasing MTZ removal percentages with rising NCs concentration up to 30 mg/L. However, concentrations 40 mg/L and beyond impeded the degradation rate due to increase in light scattering. This could be caused by the agglomeration and turbidity as a result of higher NCs dosage.

To comprehend the effect of MTZ levels on the photocatalytic process, a constant dosage of NCs (30 mg/L) was utilized with various MTZ levels (Supplementary material Fig. S3c and d). Up to an MTZ dosage of 20 mg/L, the photocatalytic removal rate showed a consistent increase. However, further increases in MTZ levels inhibited photodegradation due to excessive surface adsorption of competing MTZ molecules. These molecules shielded the active sites on the photocatalysts, thereby hindering the process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and ionic environment
ce:section.ce:para: The impact of pH fluctuation within a photocatalytic environment can significantly alter a range of physical and chemical parameters, including substrate adsorption and surface charge. Consequently, the degradation rate undergoes notable changes. Thus, we delved into the effects of varying pH levels from 3 to 10 on MTZ photodegradation, offering insights into the process dynamics across diverse ionic environments. This exploration is visually depicted in Supplementary materials Fig. S4 a and b, with percentages recorded as 88.2, 77.6, 53.8, 89.1, 45.1, 55.1, 45.5 and 40.2 for pH 3 to 10 respectively. At a pH of 6 in the reaction mixture, the NCs attain +ve surface charge, enhancing their attraction to MTZ molecules. This results in the saturation of adsorption sites on the photocatalyst, limiting its capacity. However, in acidic conditions, there's a shortage of hydroxide radicals crucial for degrading the excess MTZ molecules adsorbed, thus reducing photocatalytic efficiency. Conversely, when the pH exceeds 6, NCs develop a negative surface charge due to increased presence of hydroxide ions. This change reduces their affinity for unprotonated MTZ molecules in the alkaline environment, hindering the degradation process. Hence, pH 6 emerges as the optimal point for MTZ photodegradation using KS-1 NCs.

The impact of different ions on the photocatalysis was explored by conducting experiments with varied reaction mixtures (Supplementary materials Fig. S4c and d). The percentage of MTZ degradation varied significantly that are recorded as follows: 94.9 % for calcium, 97.5 % for sodium, 92.1 % for phosphate, 91.4 % for chloride, 92.0 % for nitrate, 66.4 % for carbonate, 68.9 % for sulfate, and 99.5 % for manganese ions. Sulfate and carbonate ions affected the degradation, while other ions didn't interfere. The sulfate (SO4 •−) and carbonate (CO3 2−) radicals, might interfere with the major reactive oxygen species (ROS) but also possess strong oxidation properties. Despite the obstacles faced, KS-1 NCs showcased impressive durability, retaining substantial photodegradation efficacy even amidst diverse ionic environments. This underscores their promise for application in the treatment of wastewater containing various ions.

The performance of the same photocatalyst across six consecutive cycles of MTZ photodegradation, as depicted in Supplementary materials Fig. S5, demonstrates consistency. Degradation percentages for six recycle were 96.2, 92.2, 95.4, 91.8, 92.0, and 93.9 % respectively. Furthermore, XRD and XPS analyses were conducted on the recycled NCs to assess their chemical integrity and structural stability after exposure to light for a long duration (Figs. 3 and 4a–g). These analyses confirmed that the NCs maintained their chemical structure and crystallinity thus reducing the risk of photo-corrosion. These findings validate the durability and stability of the KS-1 NCs for repeated utilization in MTZ removal.

During the photodegradation process of MTZ by KS-1 NCs, we conducted a radical scavenging experiment to determine the reactive oxygen species (ROS) involved. By introducing specific scavengers such as EDTA, AgNO3, p–BQ, and IPA, into the reaction mixture, capable of capturing distinct charge carriers including h+, e−, •O2 − and •OH respectively (Supplementary materials Fig. S6). In the presence of scavengers, degradation rates declined to 21.6, 78.8, 19.1 and 10.5 % for EDTA, AgNO3, p–BQ, and IPA respectively compared to without scavengers (96.2 %). These results highlight the prominent role of •OH and •O2 − as the primary contributors to the photodegradation mechanism. The role of h+ in degradation also can be seen in Supplementary materials Fig. S6.

Employing electrochemical spin resonance (ESR) studies, we investigated the nature and magnitude of ROS generated by the prepared photocatalysts. Under visible light conditions, a spin trapping experiment was conducted using DMPO to capture radicals. The outcomes of this experiment were graphically represented against magnetic field and intensity, as demonstrated in Supplementary materials Fig. S7. It was observed that KS-1 NCs depicts strong and prominent peaks attributed to DMPO-•O2 − and DMPO-•OH spin-adduct. The ESR results confirms the production of •O2 − and •OH in KS-1 NCs. Thereby, it can be said that the decoration of Sr2V2O7 over N-doped KNbO3 significantly contributed to the generation of radicals. Additionally, the findings from ESR provide further evidence to corroborate the conclusions drawn from the radical-scavenging experiment.

The KS-1 NCs possess to form Z-scheme heterojunction during the irradiation of visible light. The observed band gap, coupled with the alignment of the CB and VB positions, illustrates a notable resemblance to the Z-scheme heterojunction mechanism. This alignment fosters an effective charge transfer mechanism, thereby amplifying redox reactions within the system. The overall configuration supports the notion that the photocatalytic process operates through a Z-scheme heterojunction mechanism, facilitating improved charge transfer dynamics and more effective redox reactions (Fig. 7 ). For Sr₂V₂O₇, the CB value is less negative than that of KNbO₃ and is closer to the VB of KNbO₃. This indicates that photogenerated electrons (e⁻) transfer from the CB of Sr₂V₂O₇ to the VB of KNbO₃ and form Z-scheme heterojunction. As the KNbO3, CB value is more negative that the potential of O2/O2
.− (–0.33 eV), the photogenerated electron can have the ability to reduce O2 to O2
.− . The VB potential of Sr2V2O7 (2.52 eV) is more positive than the redox potential of H2O/•OH (1.99 eV) where it can possibly oxidize water to •OH.
(5) N–KNbO3 + hν → e− + h+
(6) Sr2V2O7 + hν → e− + h+
(7) H2O + h+→ OH− + H+
(8) O2 + e− → O2
.−
(9) OH− + h+ → •OH
(10) MTZ + •OH → Intermediates → CO2 + H2O
(11) MTZ + O2
.− → Intermediates → CO2 + H2O

Hence, the strong redox potential of e− in KNbO3 and h+ in Sr2V2O7 are retained to generate O2
.− and •OH respectively. Therefore, the results revealed that these free radicals were generated under visible light irradiation and were primarily contributing to the photocatalytic degradation of MTZ.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible photocatalytic degradation pathway of MTZ
ce:section.ce:para: The GC-MS/MS results revealed five intermediates and three possible degradation pathways of MTZ (Fig. 8 ). In accordance with its own structural characteristics, MTZ has a relative molecular mass (m/z = 171) and its degradation occurred primarily through N-de-nitration, nitro-reduction, hydroxyethyl cleavage, hydrogenation, oxidation and ring-opening [32,33]. In the degradation pathway, the first step involves hydrogenation and oxidation, resulting in the formation of the initial intermediate, I1 (m/z = 156). Subsequently, ring opening reaction leading to the generation of intermediate I2 (m/z = 146). Further cleavage of I2 ultimately leads to the production of CO2 and H2O. In the second pathway, MTZ undergoes nitro-reduction and undergoes substitution with a hydroxyl group, causing ring opening. This process leads to the formation of intermediate I3 (m/z = 117). The delineation of these pathways provides insight into the sequential transformations and the identification of key intermediates during the degradation of MTZ. In the third pathway, MTZ undergoes hydroxylation and de-nitration leads to the formation of I4 (m/z = 101), followed by the hydroxyethyl cleavage to form I5 (m/z = 91) and these intermediate mineralized into low toxicity species (CO2, and H2O).

Following GC-MS/MS analysis, the intermediates were subjected to evaluations of both acute and chronic toxicity across a range of model organisms. This evaluation was conducted using the ecological structure activity relationships (ECOSAR) software database, which provides insights into the potential environmental impact of the identified compounds. This approach allowed for a systematic analysis of the toxicity profiles, aiding in the understanding of the potential ecological risks associated with the intermediates identified in this study. The researchers investigated the ecological impact of these compounds by determining their potency in specific organisms. They measured the EC50 and IC50 and the risk associated with these compounds was categorized into four levels: very toxic, toxic, harmful, and not harmful. These categories were defined based on EC50 values, with substances falling below 1 mg/L considered very toxic, between 1 and 10 mg/L classified as toxic, between 10 and 100 mg/L as harmful, and values exceeding 100 mg/L considered not harmful. The assessment involved the determination of EC50/IC50 values in fish, daphnia, and green algae to comprehensively evaluate the potential impact on these organisms. The toxicity assessments reveal a gradient in the acute toxicity of the intermediates produced during the degradation of MTZ, allowing them to be ranked from the most toxic to the least as follows I4>I1>MTZ>I3 >I5 >I2in fish, I4>I1>MTZ>I3>I5>I2 in daphnia and I4>I1>MTZ>I3>I5>I2 in green algae (Supplementary materials Fig. S8a). Similarly, the chronic toxicity of MTZ intermediates can be assessed from most toxic to the least as follows, I4>I1>MTZ>I3>I5>I2 in fish, I4 >I1>MTZ>I3> I5>I2 in daphnia and I4 >I1>MTZ>I5>I3>I2 in green algae (Supplementary materials Fig. S8b). From the result it is evident that the original pollutants are highly toxic, and while certain intermediates also pose harm. Hence, it is imperative to remove them from the environment and the resultant end-product formed during the degradation of MTZ does not exert any adverse effects on the environment.

This study emphasizes the photocatalytic capability of ternary KS-1 NCs in degrading MTZ under visible light irradiation. The investigation includes the synthesis and characterization of KNbO3, Sr2V2O7, and KS-1 NCs, and confirms the successful fabrication of a Z-scheme heterojunction in KS-1 NCs. This heterojunction facilitates efficient charge separation, amplifies light absorption, and boosts ROS generation than the counter NPs. HR-TEM and SEM analyses provided detailed insights into the morphology of the NCs, revealing a uniform distribution of particles without any signs of agglomeration. To determine the structural crystallinity of the synthesized materials, XRD analysis was conducted, which confirmed the successful synthesis of KS-1 NCs and the formation of their heterojunction structure. The optical and photoluminescence properties of the NCs were evaluated through UV–vis DRS and PL spectroscopy. These analyses demonstrated that NCs possess enhanced light-absorbing capabilities and exhibit reduced charge recombination rates compared to the pristine nanoparticles. The photocatalytic degradation of MTZ using KS NCs achieved a remarkable 96.2 % degradation in 140 min and the optimal results were observed at a pH of 6. Additionally, the presence of ions in the solution did not diminish the efficacy of the KS NCs. The recyclability of NCs was analysed by conducting multiple MTZ degradation cycle which highlighted their potential and practical application. Moreover, the consistent stability of KS-1 NCs across multiple MTZ degradation cycles underscores their practical applicability. Predictions of toxicity using ECOSAR indicate reduced toxicity in end products, thereby emphasizing the potential of KS-1 NCs in elimination of organic pollutants in water.

S. Sudheer Khan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition. V. Subhiksha: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. G. Harini: Methodology, Investigation. Asad Syed: Formal analysis, Funding acquisition. B. Janani: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. J.P. Steffy: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Abdallah M. Elgorban: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Islem Abid: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Ling Shing Wong: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors extend their appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSPD2024R745), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2024.104610.
